frameworks.name: serial dependence
frameworks.one_line: current perception biased by recently seen stimuli toward positive temporal correlations

frameworks.name: perceptual decision-making
frameworks.one_line: orientation discrimination task at threshold contrast as decision frame

frameworks.name: natural image statistics
frameworks.one_line: slow temporal regularities cited as ecological motivation for serial bias

core_question: Why do individuals differ in serial dependence — attractive, repulsive, or none — in perceptual decision-making?
key_assumptions: physical stimulus, percept, and motor response are dissociable sources of serial bias
stimulus-response mapping can be manipulated to isolate motor contribution
orientation at threshold yields measurable trial-to-trial bias
manipulations: stimulus-response mapping: consistent vs random across experiments
within-subject task with vs without choice-response coupling
key_findings: stimulus percept (not the physical stimulus per se) attracts subsequent perceptual choices
motor responses induce a repulsive serial effect
with consistent choice-response mapping, individuals show positive, repulsive, or no overall bias
interpretations: individual differences in serial dependence arise from opposing influences of perceptual choices (attractive) and motor responses (repulsive)
typical mixed mapping in past studies confounds these two opposing sources
